[Tool: Edit] {"file_path":"/home/runner/work/erk/erk/tests/tui/app/test_streaming.py","old_string":"    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_timeout_fires_and_kills_process(self, tmp_path: Path) -> None:
        """Timeout kills subprocess and shows error message.

        Uses a short timeout (0.1s) with a sleep command to verify
        the timeout handler fires and terminates the process.
        """
        provider = FakePlanDataProvider(
            plans=[make_plan_row(123, "Test Plan")],
            repo_root=tmp_path,
        )
        filters = PlanFilters.default()
        app = ErkDashApp(provider=provider, service=FakePlanService(), filters=filters, refresh_interval=0)","new_string":"    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_timeout_fires_and_kills_process(self, tmp_path: Path) -> None:
        """Timeout kills subprocess and shows error message.

        Uses a short timeout (0.1s) with a sleep command to verify
        the timeout handler fires and terminates the process.
        """
        provider = FakePlanDataProvider(
            plans=[make_plan_row(123, "Test Plan")],
        )
        filters = PlanFilters.default()
        app = ErkDashApp(provider=provider, service=FakePlanService(repo_root=tmp_path), filters=filters, refresh_interval=0)"}

session.assistant: All 6 agents completed successfully. Now let me handle the remaining issues. Let me also check the close_plan_in_process test that was failing and the line-length issues, then re-run CI:
session.tool_result:
197→ async def test_close_plan_in_process_creates_output_panel(self) -> None: 198→ """In-process close plan creates and mounts output panel.""" 199→ provider = FakePlanDataProvider( 200→ plans=[ 201→ make_plan_row( 202→ 123, 203→ "Test Plan", 204→ plan_url="https://github.com/test/repo/issues/123", 205→ ) 206→ ], 207→ ) 208→ filters = PlanFilters.default() 209→ app = ErkDashApp(provider=provider, service=FakePlanService(), filters=filters, refresh_interval=0) 210→ 211→ async with app.run_test() as pilot: 212→ await pilot.pause() 213→ await pilot.pause() 214→ 215→ # Open detail screen 216→ await pilot.press("space") 217→ await pilot.pause() 218→ await pilot.pause() 219→ 220→ detail_screen = app.screen_stack[-1] 221→ assert isinstance(detail_screen, PlanDetailScreen) 222→ 223→ # Run close plan in-process 224→ detail_screen.run_close_plan_in_process(123, "https://github.com/test/repo/issues/123") 225→ 226→ # Output panel should be created
... [49 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: This test calls `detail_screen.run_close_plan_in_process(...)` which internally uses `self._service.close_plan(...)`. The `FakePlanService()` default `close_plan` returns empty list (it's defined in `FakePlanService`). But then the test expects `provider._plans` to have the plan removed. Let me look at what `run_close_plan_in_process` does:
session.tool_result: src/erk/tui/screens/plan_detail_screen.py:469: def run_close_plan_in_process(

469→ def run_close_plan_in_process( 470→ self, 471→ plan_id: int, 472→ plan_url: str, 473→ ) -> None: 474→ """Run close plan in-process using HTTP client directly. 475→ 476→ This is much faster than spawning a subprocess because it uses 477→ the HTTP client that was initialized at TUI startup. 478→ 479→ Args: 480→ plan_id: The plan identifier to close 481→ plan_url: The plan URL for PR linkage lookup 482→ """ 483→ # Create and mount output panel 484→ title = f"Closing Plan #{plan_id}" 485→ self._output_panel = CommandOutputPanel(title) 486→ dialog = self.query_one("#detail-dialog") 487→ dialog.mount(self._output_panel) 488→ self._command_running = True 489→ 490→ # Run in-process worker (no timeout needed - HTTP has its own timeout) 491→ self._run_close_plan_worker(plan_id, plan_url) 492→ 493→ @work(thread=True) 494→ def _run_close_plan_worker( 495→ self, 496→ plan_id: int, 497→ plan_url: str, 498→ ) -> None:
... [35 lines omitted] ...
511→ # Error boundary: catch all exceptions from HTTP operations to display 522→ except Exception as e: 523→ self.app.call_from_thread(panel.append_line, f"Error: {e}")
session.assistant: I see - it uses `self._executor.close_plan(...)`. Let me check what `_executor` is:

session.assistant: Let me look at how the executor is constructed - where PlanDetailScreen gets its executor:
session.tool_result: src/erk/tui/actions/navigation.py:116: PlanDetailScreen( src/erk/tui/screens/plan_detail_screen.py:43:class PlanDetailScreen(ModalScreen):

105→ # Create executor with injected dependencies 106→ executor = RealCommandExecutor( 107→ browser_launch=self._service.browser.launch, 108→ clipboard_copy=self._service.clipboard.copy, 109→ close_plan_fn=self._service.close_plan, 110→ notify_fn=self._notify_with_severity, 111→ refresh_fn=self.action_refresh, 112→ dispatch_to_queue_fn=self._service.dispatch_to_queue, 113→ ) 114→ 115→ self.push_screen( 116→ PlanDetailScreen( 117→ row=row, 118→ clipboard=self._service.clipboard, 119→ browser=self._service.browser, 120→ executor=executor, 121→ repo_root=self._service.repo_root, 122→ view_mode=self._view_mode, 123→ cmux_integration=self._cmux_integration, 124→ ) 125→ ) 126→ 127→ def action_view_plan_body(self: ErkDashApp) -> None: 128→ """Display the plan/objective content in a modal (fetched on-demand).""" 129→ row = self._get_selected_row()
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>
session.assistant:
I see - the executor's `close_plan_fn=self._service.close_plan` is set. In the test, when `run_close_plan_in_process` is called, it uses `self._executor.close_plan(...)`. The executor is created when the detail screen is opened via `action_view_plan_detail`, which constructs a `RealCommandExecutor` with `close_plan_fn=self._service.close_plan`.
The test opens the detail screen by pressing "space", which triggers this code path. But the `FakePlanService().close_plan(...)` returns `[]` (empty list). The test then checks `len(provider._plans) == 1` - it expects the plan to be removed from the provider's internal list. But `close_plan` on `FakePlanService` doesn't modify the provider at all.
This was probably working before because the old combined `FakePlanDataProvider` had `close_plan` that also removed the plan from `_plans`. Now `close_plan` is on `FakePlanService` which has no reference to the provider's `_plans`. The test failure is expected.
Let me look at how close_plan removal worked in the old combined provider:
session.tool_result:
50→ self._fetch_error = fetch_error 51→ self._plan_content_by_plan_id: dict[int, str] = {} 52→ self._objective_content_by_plan_id: dict[int, str] = {} 53→ self._review_threads_by_pr: dict[int, list[PRReviewThread]] = {} 54→ self._check_runs_by_pr: dict[int, list[PRCheckRun]] = {} 55→ self._ci_summaries_by_pr: dict[int, dict[str, str]] = {} 56→ self._stacks_by_branch: dict[str, list[str]] = {} 57→ 58→ @property 59→ def repo_root(self) -> Path: 60→ """Get the repository root path.""" 61→ return self._repo_root 62→ 63→ @property 64→ def clipboard(self) -> Clipboard: 65→ """Get the clipboard interface for copy operations.""" 66→ return self._clipboard 67→ 68→ @property 69→ def browser(self) -> BrowserLauncher: 70→ """Get the browser launcher interface for opening URLs.""" 71→ return self._browser 72→ 73→ def fetch_plans(self, filters: PlanFilters) -> tuple[list[PlanRowData], FetchTimings | None]: 74→ """Return canned plan data. 75→ 76→ Args: 77→ filters: Checks plans_by_labels first using filter labels, 78→ falls back to default plans list. 79→
... [5 lines omitted] ...
106: def close_plan(self, plan_id: int, plan_url: str) -> list[int]:

106→ def close_plan(self, plan_id: int, plan_url: str) -> list[int]: 107→ """Fake close plan implementation. 108→ 109→ Removes the plan from the internal list and tracks the closure. 110→ 111→ Args: 112→ plan_id: The plan ID to close 113→ plan_url: The plan URL (unused in fake) 114→ 115→ Returns: 116→ Empty list (no PRs closed in fake) 117→ """ 118→ self._plans = [p for p in self._plans if p.plan_id != plan_id] 119→ return [] 120→ 121→ def dispatch_to_queue(self, plan_id: int, plan_url: str) -> None: 122→ """Fake dispatch to queue implementation. 123→ 124→ Tracks the dispatch without actually dispatching. 125→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>
session.assistant:
The old combined `FakePlanDataProvider.close_plan()` removes the plan from `self._plans`. But now close_plan is on `FakePlanService` which has no `_plans`. The test was checking `provider._plans` length - it worked because the old monolithic class had both the plan list AND close_plan on the same object.
Now with the split, the `RealCommandExecutor`'s `close_plan_fn` is `self._service.close_plan` which doesn't modify the provider's `_plans`. Then the old combined `FakePlanDataProvider` in erk_shared (the one the test imports) still has `close_plan` that removes from `_plans`, but it's not the one being used as the executor's `close_plan_fn` - the executor uses `service.close_plan`.
The fix: this test's behavior needs to work differently. The `refresh_fn` in the executor should trigger a refresh which re-fetches plans. But this is in-process testing with fakes. The cleanest fix is to make the test verify the close_plan behavior differently - or ensure the executor's close_plan_fn points to a function that also removes the plan from the provider.
Actually, looking at the production flow: after `close_plan` succeeds (line 515), the executor's `refresh_fn` is called (from `_command_completed` handler). Let me check the executor's close_plan flow more carefully:
session.tool_result:
420→ if self._command_running: 421→ return 422→ 423→ # If panel exists and completed, refresh data if successful 424→ if self._output_panel is not None: 425→ if self._output_panel.is_completed: 426→ if self._executor and self._output_panel.succeeded: 427→ self._executor.refresh_data() 428→ await self._flush_next_callbacks() 429→ self.dismiss(result) 430→ return 431→ 432→ # Normal dismiss 433→ await self._flush_next_callbacks() 434→ self.dismiss(result)
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>
session.assistant:
I see - the refresh happens only when the user dismisses the modal. But in the old code, the combined `FakePlanDataProvider.close_plan()` removed the plan from `_plans` directly. The test expects `provider._plans` to shrink after close_plan_in_process runs.
The issue is that in the new architecture, the `executor.close_plan_fn` calls `service.close_plan()` which doesn't touch the provider's plan list. The provider's plan list is only updated on refresh (which re-fetches from the provider).
The simplest fix: the old combined `FakePlanDataProvider` (still used by these tests) has both `close_plan` (which removes from `_plans`) AND the TUI data provider methods. The test still uses this combined class. The problem is that the `RealCommandExecutor` is constructed with `close_plan_fn=self._service.close_plan`, but `self._service` is `FakePlanService()` (the new separate class) which doesn't modify `_plans`.
The cleanest approach: the test should set up the `FakePlanService` so its `close_plan` behavior matches. But `FakePlanService.close_plan` just returns `[]`. I could add a hook or I could rethink this.
Actually, a better approach: the test just needs to verify different things now. Instead of checking `provider._plans` changed, it should verify that `service.close_plan` was called. Or I can make the test work by having the old combined `FakePlanDataProvider` be used as the service too, since it implements both interfaces. But that defeats the purpose of the split.
The simplest correct fix: update the test to verify the close_plan behavior through the service, not the provider. Let me also check how the test invokes the flow to understand what executor.close_plan_fn actually calls:
The test presses "space" to open detail screen, which calls `action_view_plan_detail`, which creates `RealCommandExecutor(close_plan_fn=self._service.close_plan, ...)`. So the test's `FakePlanService().close_plan()` gets called. This returns `[]` but doesn't remove anything from `provider._plans`.
I think the best fix is to update the test assertion. Instead of checking `provider._plans` changed, check that the close operation completed successfully (output panel shows success):

tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param: @pytest.mark.asyncio async def test_close_plan_in_process_completes_successfully(s...[truncated 1095 chars]... not None assert panel.is_completed assert panel.succeeded is True

session.assistant: This is a cmux focus test. Let me check if `_cmux_focus_workspace` uses `repo_root`:
session.tool_result: src/erk/tui/operations/workers.py:163: def _cmux_focus_workspace(self: ErkDashApp, branch: str) -> None:

163→ def _cmux_focus_workspace(self: ErkDashApp, branch: str) -> None: 164→ """Focus the cmux workspace matching a branch name. 165→ 166→ Lists cmux workspaces, finds one whose title matches the branch, 167→ and selects it. Both cmux calls are fast Unix socket operations. 168→ Called from a background thread -- does not need @work decorator. 169→ 170→ Args: 171→ branch: The branch name to match against workspace titles 172→ """ 173→ import shutil 174→ 175→ if shutil.which("cmux") is None: 176→ return 177→ 178→ list_result = subprocess.run( 179→ ["cmux", "--json", "list-workspaces"], 180→ capture_output=True, 181→ text=True, 182→ stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL, 183→ ) 184→ if list_result.returncode != 0 or not list_result.stdout: 185→ return 186→ 187→ try: 188→ data = json.loads(list_result.stdout) 189→ except json.JSONDecodeError: 190→ return 191→ 192→ if not isinstance(data, dict):
... [5 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: The `_cmux_focus_workspace` method uses `subprocess.run` directly and checks `shutil.which("cmux")`. The test monkeypatches `subprocess.run` but not `shutil.which`. In CI, `cmux` won't be found by `shutil.which`, so the function returns early at line 176. This is probably a pre-existing issue that's flaky in CI. Let me check if it was passing before by checking master:

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: Check last commit on this test file

session.tool_result: a2192b135 Rename cmux sync → cmux checkout (#8790)

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: git diff master -- tests/tui/app/test_async_operations.py 2>/dev/null | grep -c "^[-+]" | head -5

session.assistant: Let me check the original test on master to see if it had `shutil.which` monkeypatched:
session.tool_result:
async def test_focus_selects_matching_workspace( self, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, tmp_path: Path ) -> None: """Should call select-workspace when a matching workspace is found.""" import json import subprocess
provider = FakePlanDataProvider( plans=[make_plan_row(123, "Test Plan", pr_number=456, pr_head_branch="test-branch")], repo_root=tmp_path, ) filters = PlanFilters.default() app = ErkDashApp(provider=provider, filters=filters, refresh_interval=0)
captured_run_calls: list[list[str]] = []
def fake_run(*args: object, **kwargs: object) -> subprocess.CompletedProcess[str]: cmd = list(args[0]) if args else [] # type: ignore[arg-type] captured_run_calls.append(cmd) if "list-workspaces" in cmd: data = json.dumps({"workspaces": [{"title": "test-branch", "ref": "ws-42"}]}) return subprocess.CompletedProcess(args=cmd, returncode=0, stdout=data, stderr="") return subprocess.CompletedProcess(args=cmd, returncode=0, stdout="", stderr="")
monkeypatch.setattr(subprocess, "run", fake_run)
async with app.run_test() as pilot: await pilot.pause() await pilot.pause()
